coaching.subject: machine-executable assertion for gate test AC3

coaching.coaching: Plan how full export surface of applier.py re-exports will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'verified by reading the gate test' in AC3 with a concrete runnable assertion — e.g. `grep -q '556a-5a1f-adb3-4139' tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py` — so the ticket-id and rationale requirement is machine-checkable.
evidence: 'verified by reading the gate test' — no grep, assert, or CI-runnable check is specified

The gate test running green only proves the exemption LIST is correct (inbound_fields.py is allowed), not that the rationale string and follow-up ticket id '556a-5a1f-adb3-4139' appear in the test source
findings.finding: AC3's proving method 'verified by reading the gate test' is not a machine-executable command — it relies on human visual inspection of the test file rather than a runnable assertion that fails automatically if the exemption, rationale, or ticket id are absent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'The sweep gate documents the single inbound_fields.py exemption with its rationale and the follow-up ticket id — verified by reading the gate test.'
scenarios: An executor writes the exemption as a bare string constant with no comment, satisfying the sweep assertion but failing the rationale/ticket-id documentation requirement — AC3 is silently unsatisfied while CI stays green
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'verified by `grep -q "556a-5a1f-adb3-4139" tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py && grep -q "inbound_fields.py" tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py` passing in CI.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `applier.py` to scope and update its `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`/`_AdfModule_Applier`/`_load_adf_module`/`_normalize_adf_body` imports and `__all__` entries to reference the replacement symbols (or drop the re-exports if no downstream consumer resolves them via `applier.<name>`).
evidence: applier.py:111: `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER,`
applier.py:119: `_AdfModule_Applier,`
applier.py:124: `_load_adf_module,`
applier.py:125: `_normalize_adf_body,`
applier.py __all__ at lines 322, 326, 361, 367 re-exports these symbols
Scope section does not list applier.py as an edit target
Plan says 'delete the literal key at inbound_translate.py:315' and replace `_normalize_adf_body` routing — both symbols applier.py imports
findings.finding: `applier.py` imports `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`, `_AdfModule_Applier`, `_load_adf_module`, and `_normalize_adf_body` from `inbound_translate` at module scope (lines 111, 119, 124, 125) and re-exports all four in `__all__` (lines 322, 326, 361, 367). When the plan deletes `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`/`_AdfModule_Applier` and replaces `_normalize_adf_body` in `inbound_translate.py`, `applier.py`'s import block will raise `ImportError` at load time. `applier.py` is not in the plan's scope list.
scenarios: After this ticket's changes, `import rebar_reconciler.applier` fails with ImportError on the deleted symbols, breaking every downstream test and runtime path that loads the applier.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `apply_inbound_records.py` to scope (or confirm `_normalize_adf_body` is kept as a backward-compatible shim) and update its three `_normalize_adf_body` call sites to use the backend's `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text`.
evidence: apply_inbound_records.py:35: `_normalize_adf_body,`
apply_inbound_records.py:145: `'description': _normalize_adf_body(fields.get('description'))`
apply_inbound_records.py:268: `body_text = _normalize_adf_body(jc.get('body'))`
apply_inbound_records.py:308: `'description': lambda v: _normalize_adf_body(v) if isinstance(v, dict) else v`
Scope section does not list apply_inbound_records.py as an edit target
Plan targets `inbound_translate.py` for deletion of ADF literal key and routing `_normalize_adf_body` through InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text
findings.finding: `apply_inbound_records.py` imports `_normalize_adf_body` from `inbound_translate` (line 35) and calls it at three sites (lines 145, 268, 308). When `inbound_translate.py` removes or renames `_normalize_adf_body` as the plan requires, `apply_inbound_records.py` will fail with ImportError or NameError. It is not in the plan's scope list.
scenarios: If `_normalize_adf_body` is deleted or its signature changes in `inbound_translate.py`, `apply_inbound_records.py` breaks at import time; the bug 1bb2-5da5 defense-in-depth is also lost at the apply-records level without an explicit update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `applier.py` to Scope: remove the now-dead `_load_adf_module` import at line 124 and the `"_load_adf_module"` entry from `__all__` at line 361 (or confirm the function is kept as a thin shim/deprecated stub if external callers rely on the re-export).
evidence: Plan Scope lists: outbound_comments.py, inbound_translate.py, _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, inbound_fields.py, tests — applier.py is absent
applier.py:124: `_load_adf_module,` (module-level import from inbound_translate)
applier.py:361: `"_load_adf_module",` (re-export in __all__)
inbound_translate.py:319-324: _load_adf_module is the function that uses _ADF_KEY_APPLIER — deleting the key implies deleting or hollowing out this function
Plan Approach: 'the literal key at inbound_translate.py:315 is deleted'
findings.finding: applier.py imports _load_adf_module from inbound_translate at module scope (line 124) and re-exports it in __all__ (line 361), but the plan deletes _ADF_KEY_APPLIER (inbound_translate:315) and its sole consumer _load_adf_module (lines 319-324) without listing applier.py in Scope — producing a broken module-level ImportError when applier is loaded after this change.
scenarios: Any code path that imports applier (reconcile.py, tests) will fail with ImportError when applier.py:124 tries `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate import _load_adf_module` after _load_adf_module has been removed
findings.suggested_fix: Add applier.py to the Scope file list. In the implementation, after routing _normalize_adf_body through the inbound mapper, either (a) delete _load_adf_module from inbound_translate.py AND remove its import/re-export from applier.py, or (b) keep _load_adf_module as a private helper in inbound_translate.py (it still loads the adf module, just not called by _normalize_adf_body) — but the simpler path is (a) since no external code calls applier._load_adf_module.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the Testing section how the byte-identity baseline is established — e.g. 'expected output strings are extracted from the existing adapters/jira/comment_limits.py and adf.py behavior and encoded as explicit fixture constants in the new tests BEFORE the lazy keys are deleted.'
evidence: 'byte-identically to today' implies comparison to the pre-refactor output
The old path (adapters.jira.adf and adapters.jira.comment_limits loaded via lazy keys) is deleted by this story, so a live side-by-side comparison is impossible post-landing
No mention of golden fixtures or how expected byte-sequences are captured before deletion
findings.finding: The Testing section requires 'byte-identically to today' for the outbound comment formatting/truncation path, but the old code path (the lazy-loaded vendor modules being deleted) will no longer exist to serve as a live comparison baseline — the plan does not specify how the byte-identity baseline is established (golden fixture values, parametrized comparison against extracted expected strings, etc.).
findings.location: Testing — 'Happy path: an outbound comment body formats and truncates byte-identically to today via the sanitizer route'
scenarios: An executor writes a test that calls only the new path and asserts non-empty output — CI stays green but byte-identity with the old path is never verified
A subtle truncation-boundary change in the new FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment implementation goes undetected because the baseline was never encoded
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Testing section: 'Before deleting the lazy keys, run the existing truncation/ADF-encode paths against a representative set of inputs and record their outputs as fixture constants. The new behavior tests assert exact equality to those recorded constants.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out / expand-contract note to the plan covering the Protocol additions to `FieldSanitizer` and `InboundMapper` — either state that both Protocol and all implementations land atomically in a single commit (making rollback a simple revert), or specify a two-step expand-contract sequence.
evidence: 'FieldSanitizer ... gains the comment-fit surface' — new Protocol obligation, no back-out path stated
'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str' — new required Protocol member added without expand-contract or rollback note
The plan has no section addressing what happens if the protocol-extension commit lands but the implementation commit does not (e.g. in a partial merge or bisect scenario)
findings.finding: The plan adds new required methods to `FieldSanitizer` and `InboundMapper` Protocols without stating an explicit back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing. If implementation is partial (e.g. Protocol definition updated in `_backend.py` before `adapters/jira/backend.py` and `inbound_fields.py` implementations land), type-check or runtime failures would have no documented rollback path.
findings.location: Approach (FieldSanitizer / InboundMapper protocol additions); Scope (_backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, inbound_fields.py)
scenarios: A bisect or partial-merge scenario where _backend.py Protocol is updated but adapters/jira/backend.py is not yet would cause type-check failures and potential AttributeError at runtime with no documented recovery path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence under Approach or a new 'Rollback' subsection: 'Protocol additions to `FieldSanitizer` and `InboundMapper` and their sole Jira implementations land in a single atomic commit; rollback is a plain `git revert` of that commit with no data migration required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a design note (ADR entry, AGENTS.md section, or inline protocol docstring in `_backend.py`) documenting that `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` is intentionally dual-purpose — serving both the inbound apply path and the outbound comment-diff ADF-decode path — and must handle both dict and string inputs for any backend implementation.
evidence: 'normalize_rich_text therefore serves BOTH the inbound apply path and the outbound comment-diff decode'
No ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc file appears in Scope or Testing
The dual-purpose nature violates the principle of least surprise for future maintainers extending InboundMapper
findings.finding: The plan introduces a non-obvious dual-purpose design for `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` — it serves both the inbound apply path (bug 1bb2-5da5 defense) AND the outbound comment-diff decode path (bug 85a1). This is a novel architectural decision about interface responsibility that is not captured in any ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update listed in Scope.
findings.location: Approach section (InboundMapper paragraph + Plan-review remediation block); Scope section — _backend.py
scenarios: A future contributor adding a new backend implements `InboundMapper` and does not realize `normalize_rich_text` must handle both decode contexts, breaking outbound comment dedup silently.
A reviewer of `outbound_comments.py` sees an `InboundMapper` call and assumes it is a mistake, reverting or refactoring it incorrectly.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_backend.py`, add an explicit docstring to the `normalize_rich_text` protocol method that states: 'Used on BOTH the inbound apply path (defense against raw vendor rich-text in EDIT events) and the outbound comment-diff path (decoding remote ADF bodies before text comparison). Implementations must handle: dict → decoded text (vendor rich-text), str → identity. See epic bbf1 / bug 85a1 / bug 1bb2-5da5.' Reference this docstring from the Scope entry for `_backend.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the 4 existing `_normalize_adf_body` call sites in `apply_inbound_records.py` (lines 145, 268, 308) and `applier.py` (re-export) should also be routed through `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` to reach the Rule-of-Three threshold and make the Protocol boundary load-bearing — or explicitly document why those callers legitimately stay on the Jira-specific helper outside the port.
evidence: Plan: '`InboundMapper` gains `normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str`' — a new Protocol member
apply_inbound_records.py:145 `_normalize_adf_body(fields.get('description'))` — not in Scope
apply_inbound_records.py:268 `body_text = _normalize_adf_body(jc.get('body'))` — not in Scope
apply_inbound_records.py:308 `'description': lambda v: _normalize_adf_body(v) if isinstance(v, dict) else v` — not in Scope
applier.py:367 `'_normalize_adf_body'` in __all__ re-export — not in Scope
Plan Scope lists only `inbound_translate.py` (1 site) and implicitly `outbound_comments.py` (1 site) as the rerouted callers — total 2 call sites
findings.finding: `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` is a new Protocol method that will have at most 2 call sites post-landing (`inbound_translate.py` and `outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body`), while 4 equivalent `_normalize_adf_body` call sites in `apply_inbound_records.py` (lines 145, 268, 308) and `applier.py` (re-export at line 367) remain outside the Protocol — below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a new interface method and leaving the Protocol's coverage inconsistent.
findings.location: Approach section / _backend.py InboundMapper Protocol / Scope: inbound_fields.py
scenarios: A future backend author implementing `InboundMapper` must add `normalize_rich_text` but the dominant call path (apply_inbound_records.py + applier.py) still calls the Jira-specific `_normalize_adf_body` directly — the Protocol boundary is not load-bearing for the majority of callers, undermining its stated backend-neutrality goal.

If a second backend lands and its `normalize_rich_text` is never exercised by `apply_inbound_records.py`, inbound CREATE and EDIT description normalization will still run Jira ADF logic for that backend.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `apply_inbound_records.py` and its `applier.py` re-export to the Scope (routing their `_normalize_adf_body` calls through the injected backend's `inbound.normalize_rich_text`), which would bring call-site count to 5-6 and make the neutrality win complete; or demote `normalize_rich_text` from a Protocol method to a module-level helper in `inbound_fields.py` shared by all callers without expanding the Protocol surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the sweep-gate exemption list with the actual baseline: verify whether outbound_links.py (line 30) and outbound_differ.py (line 39) contain 'rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira' literals that must either be additional named exemptions in the gate or be neutralized by this story (or sibling stories 625b/eefd), and update the plan's exemption count and rationale accordingly.
evidence: outbound_links.py:30: 'from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.jira_fields import _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' — a package-root core module with the literal
outbound_differ.py:39: 'from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.outbound_fields import ...' — a package-root core module with the literal
test_backend_neutrality.py:144: 'outbound_differ.py / outbound_links.py are NOT covered here: their vendor field/link helpers are neutralized by sibling stories 625b / eefd'
The plan specifies exactly ONE exemption (inbound_fields.py:24); two additional core files are unaccounted for
findings.finding: The plan asserts the new sweep case in test_backend_neutrality.py will have 'a single recorded exemption for inbound_fields.py:24', but the current codebase contains at least two other package-root core modules that contain the literal string 'rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira': outbound_links.py:30 (a direct import) and outbound_differ.py:39 (a direct import). The existing test_backend_neutrality.py explicitly notes these two files are out-of-scope for the current zero-adapter-import gate ('neutralized by sibling stories 625b / eefd'). As written, the new sweep gate would either fail on delivery (hitting outbound_links.py and outbound_differ.py) or silently require additional undocumented exemptions beyond the single one the plan specifies.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Add a sweep case to tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py: no package-root core module contains the literal string "rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira", with a single recorded exemption for inbound_fields.py:24'
scenarios: The sweep gate is written exactly as specified and fails CI on delivery because outbound_links.py and outbound_differ.py contain the forbidden literal
The implementer adds silent undocumented exemptions for outbound_links.py and outbound_differ.py, weakening the gate and diverging from AC #3 ('verified by reading the gate test')
findings.suggested_fix: Enumerate all package-root core modules that currently contain the 'rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira' literal (outbound_links.py, outbound_differ.py, inbound_fields.py, plus outbound_comments.py and inbound_translate.py which this story removes). Declare each non-removed file as a named, rationale-documented exemption in the sweep gate, referencing the sibling story (625b/eefd) that will clear the remaining ones. This makes AC #3 ('verified by reading the gate test') accurate.

findings.finding: The plan routes outbound_comments.py's _ADF_KEY usage through sanitizer.sanitize_comment, but sanitize_comment has signature (body: str) -> str and cannot decode ADF-dict typed Jira comment bodies — the ADF-dict→text leg of _normalize_comment_body (outbound_comments.py:133-134) is silently left unaddressed, so _ADF_KEY cannot actually be deleted.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the plan's Approach section to note that _normalize_comment_body's ADF-decode responsibility (for Jira comment bodies arriving as dicts) is routed through `backend.inbound.normalize_rich_text(body)`, making the Jira comment bodies text before the truncation/comparison step — consistent with InboundMapper's existing role of decoding vendor-shaped payloads. This keeps sanitize_comment's signature as (body: str) -> str and naturally eliminates both _ADF_KEY and _COMMENT_LIMITS_KEY from outbound_comments.py.

findings.finding: outbound_comments.py's _ADF_KEY (line 68) also backs an ADF-DECODE call at _normalize_comment_body:134 (adf_to_text on inbound Jira comment bodies), but the plan routes the outbound comment path only through FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment (fit/truncate) and does not name which port role replaces this decode when the key is deleted.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
